nativeRange.habitat: Shallow coastal zones including estuaries, seagrass beds, mangrove swamps, and reef flats; typically found at depths of 3–30 m

diet.notes: Has been documented feeding on molluscs, crustaceans, worms, and small invertebrates. In captivity, tends to accept chopped shrimp, clams, mussels, squid, and krill. Hard-shelled prey such as clams and crabs are generally advised to help wear down the species' continuously growing beak-like teeth.

summary: The Immaculatus Puffer is a mid-sized Indo-Pacific puffer (Arothron immaculatus) with a milky white body and tan-brown markings, generally considered one of the milder puffer species but still semi-aggressive and best kept singly with large, robust tankmates in a fish-only system.
careNotes:
The Immaculatus Puffer is distributed across the Indo-West Pacific from the Red Sea and East Africa through Indonesia and southern Japan. In the wild, it tends to inhabit shallow coastal zones including estuaries, mangrove swamps, and seagrass beds. Juveniles have been reported to tolerate reduced salinities — some sources market them as 'brackish' specimens — but adults are more accurately described as marine fish and are generally kept at full marine salinity in aquariums.

The species has a distinctive beak-like structure formed by fused teeth that continues to grow throughout life. Hard-shelled foods such as clams, mussels, and crabs are generally advised to provide the abrasion needed to prevent dental overgrowth. Body coloration is typically milky white with tan and brown overtones and yellow highlights on the fins, mouth, and eyes; the species has been reported to shift its coloration based on mood or stress.

The Immaculatus Puffer is regarded by many hobbyists as one of the more personable and approachable puffer species, and individuals have been known to recognize their owners. Despite this relatively mild reputation among puffers, it will consume invertebrates and small fish and is not considered reef-safe.

Like all members of the family Tetraodontidae (the pufferfishes), this species can inflate its body by drawing in water when threatened. Allowing it to inhale air should be avoided, as this can be harmful; transfers are generally recommended using a submerged container rather than a net. The flesh contains tetrodotoxin, a potent toxin that is also present in the skin and organs and is dangerous if consumed.

Heavy filtration and a protein skimmer are generally advised given the species' messy feeding habits.

Original manifest URL pointed to the puffers collection page; navigated to species page via search result.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked scientific name (Arothron immaculatus, Bloch & Schneider 1801), taxonomy (Tetraodontidae / Tetraodontiformes), adult size (37.5 cm TL), native range (Red Sea and East Africa to Indonesia and Japan), temperature (24.3–29.3°C), depth range (3–30 m), and diet (trophic level 3.3, omnivorous benthic feeder). Temperature range is wider than hobby sources; values averaged across all sources.

additional.name: Aqua Imports
additional.notes: Confirmed species is euryhaline but primarily marine; noted juvenile brackish tolerance (down to ~15 ppt). Provided tank size recommendation of 90 gal (340 L), temperature 75–82°F, and diet notes. Contributed to tank-size averaging.
